steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence opens with the participial phrase "Knowing that flying insects keep their backs pointed toward sunlight during the day," followed by a blank. An opening participial phrase must attach to the grammatical subject of the main clause — that subject must be the one doing the "knowing."

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'the researchers' theory is that moths mistake...': the grammatical subject is "the researchers' theory," a noun phrase — a theory cannot "know" something, so the participial phrase dangles. ✗
  • Choice B — 'the researchers theorize that moths, mistaking nighttime lights for the Sun, continually try to reorient...': "the researchers" is the subject — researchers can know things, so the participial phrase attaches correctly. ✓
  • Choice C — 'moths mistake nighttime lights for the Sun... the researchers theorize': makes "moths" the subject — moths don't "know" the fact about backs and sunlight, so this is a dangling modifier. ✗
  • Choice D — 'moths continually try to reorient their bodies... the researchers theorize, mistaking such lights for the Sun': again makes "moths" the subject after the participial phrase, creating the same dangling modifier error; the placement of "mistaking" also creates ambiguity. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
